topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The discovery happened while I was cleaning out the spare room. Tucked beneath a stack of old textbooks, I found a leather-bound journal. My hands trembled as I opened it. The handwriting was meticulous, elegant, and undeniably mine. The words detailed my mundane morning routine, the precise way I made coffee, the subtle shift in my posture when I was concentrating on a difficult task. It was all there, rendered in prose that felt both intimately known and utterly alien. A cold dread began to coil in my stomach. I flipped to a chapter, and there, describing a shared glance across a crowded room, was a line I’d never uttered. Every page felt like an invasion, a detailed map of my innermost self laid bare for public consumption. I quickly slammed the book shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet room. I stood there, breathing shallowly, my heart hammering against my ribs like a trapped bird.

[story 2]

I found the laptop open on the kitchen counter. It was a document, titled simply, "Elara." My breath hitched in my throat when I read the first paragraph. It described my laugh—that slightly off-key sound I only make when I’m truly surprised. The sentences flowed with a fluency that was breathtaking, capturing nuances of my personality I hadn't even realized were observable. A wave of nausea washed over me. I stared at the screen, my vision blurring slightly. Every word felt like a tiny, sharp needle pricking my skin. When my partner walked in, humming off-key, I tried to appear casual, picking up a dish. But my fingers were stiff, clumsy. I could feel the scrutiny of their gaze, a heavy weight pressing down on my shoulders. I wanted to disappear, to become invisible, to erase the evidence of this meticulous, consuming observation.

[story 3]

It wasn't a sudden shock; it was a slow, creeping realization. I stumbled upon a draft in a shared cloud drive—a novel. The protagonist was flawless, but the descriptions of the main character were disturbingly accurate. They detailed my anxieties, the way I chewed my lip when I was unsure, the specific shade of blue I favored when I was feeling melancholy. A profound sense of exposure settled over me, heavy and suffocating. I retreated to the bathroom, leaning against the cool tile, trying to regulate the frantic rhythm of my breathing. I looked at my reflection, searching for the person staring back. It felt like I was watching a stranger inhabiting my skin, a character whose life was being meticulously documented by the person I loved. The silence in the room amplified the frantic drumming in my chest, a relentless, unwelcome soundtrack to my unraveling.
